national.source: DGH / Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas (MoPNG); Min. Hardeep Singh Puri statements. Sedimentary basin = 3.36 million sq km across 26 basins (1.63M onland, 0.41M shallow offshore to 400m isobath, rest deepwater). ~10% under exploration historically, rising to ~16% by end-2024 after OALP awards (16% of 3.36M = ~537,600 sq km). ~1 million sq km of former 'No-Go' area (mostly in the EEZ) freed for exploration, cutting No-Go areas in the EEZ by ~99%.

oalp.rounds: OALP Rounds I-IX (2017 -> awards Apr 2025). OALP is the acreage-licensing mechanism under HELP (Hydrocarbon Exploration & Licensing Policy, 2016).
oalp.source: DGH / MoPNG. Rounds I-VIII: 144 blocks awarded, 242,055 sq km. Round IX (awarded 15 Apr 2025): 28 blocks, 136,596.45 sq km. Cumulative I-IX = 172 blocks, ~378,651 sq km (govt cited '3.78 lakh sq km'). Onshore/offshore split across the full cumulative I-IX is NOT published as a single figure; only the Round IX split is broken out (see onshore_offshore_split and OALP-IX detail below).

oalp_ix_detail.note: Some sources say 9 onland / 8 shallow / 11 ultra-deep; block-type counts vary slightly by source (13 on-land was also cited in an earlier ONGC-holdings context). Area split (onland 13,875 + shallow 26,648 + ultra-deep 96,073) = 136,596 sq km, so offshore = ~122,721 sq km (90%), onshore = ~13,875 sq km (10%) for this round.

company_wise.period: as on 31-Mar-2023 (pre-OALP-IX; grows further after Apr-2025 awards)
company_wise.source: ONGC Annual Report / DGH: ONGC held India's largest exploratory acreage = 162,701 sq km as on 31 Mar 2023. Onshore/offshore split of this total not separately captured here; historical ONGC filings (FY2020) show onshore-dominated nomination PMLs (358 PMLs, 54,321.75 sq km) + nomination PEL (5,106 sq km) + NELP PEL (21,126 sq km) + 17 OALP blocks (13 on-land, 3 shallow, 1 deepwater).

ONGC won the most blocks: 11 solo + 4 in consortium (incl. 3 with Oil India covering 10,964 sq km, and 1 Gujarat-Saurashtra shallow-water block with Reliance-BP). Per-company area totals for ONGC's OALP-IX haul not published as a single sq km figure.

Oil India: total exploration acreage rose from ~60,000 sq km to ~110,000 sq km after OALP-IX. Won 9 blocks in OALP-IX (6 solo + 3 consortium) adding >51,000 sq km, of which >47,000 sq km is deep/ultra-deep offshore. Holds 27 OALP blocks across 6 basins (16 in NE states, 5 Odisha, 3 Rajasthan, 2 Andaman, 1 Kerala-Konkan shallow offshore).
Vedanta secured 7 blocks in OALP-IX (largest among private players). Per-block area not published in the sources retrieved.

Reliance-BP secured 1 block (a Gujarat-Saurashtra basin shallow-water block) in consortium with ONGC. This was BP's maiden exploration licence in India. Signed agreement with ONGC 30 Jul 2025 to pursue Saurashtra basin exploration.

onshore_offshore_split.basis: OALP Round IX only (28 blocks, 136,596 sq km): onland 13,875.19 sq km (~10%); offshore = shallow water 26,648.14 + ultra-deepwater 96,073.12 = 122,721.26 sq km (~90%). The recent OALP rounds are heavily offshore-tilted (No-Go area opened in the EEZ). A published onshore/offshore split for the FULL cumulative OALP I-IX (378,651 sq km) or for ONGC's total 162,701 sq km acreage was NOT found in accessible sources.

producing_field_footprint.land: ~20 (anticline ~20 km long x ~4 km wide areal extent; actual surface installations occupy far less)
producing_field_footprint.unit: sq km (field areal extent, NOT surface land occupied)
producing_field_footprint.source: General references (GEM.wiki / geography sources). This is the geological field extent, not the fenced surface land footprint of well pads/facilities, which is a small fraction.

producing_field_footprint.land: 0 land; field extent ~65 km x ~23 km anticline offshore (platform-based, no onshore land occupied)
producing_field_footprint.source: Wikipedia / offshore-technology.com. Offshore field: occupies sea area via platforms, no land re-use relevance except onshore processing terminals.

key_data_points: India sedimentary basin area: 3.36 million sq km across 26 basins (1.63M sq km onland; 0.41M shallow offshore to 400m isobath; remainder deepwater). Source: DGH.

Historically ~10% of sedimentary basin was under exploration, rising to ~16% (~0.54 million sq km) by end-2024 after OALP awards. Target: 1 million sq km under exploration by 2030 (Min. Puri) -- this 1M figure is a TARGET/announcement.
~1 million sq km of former 'No-Go' area (largely in the EEZ) freed for exploration; cut No-Go areas in the EEZ by ~99%.
OALP Rounds I-VIII: 144 blocks, 242,055 sq km awarded. Round IX (15 Apr 2025): 28 blocks, 136,596 sq km. Cumulative I-IX ~= 172 blocks, ~378,651 sq km ('3.78 lakh sq km').
OALP-IX split: onland 13,875 sq km (~10%), shallow water 26,648 sq km, ultra-deepwater 96,073 sq km -> offshore ~122,721 sq km (~90%).
ONGC = India's largest exploration acreage holder: 162,701 sq km as on 31 Mar 2023 (pre-OALP-IX). Won the most OALP-IX blocks (15: 11 solo + 4 consortium).
Oil India total exploration acreage nearly doubled from ~60,000 to ~110,000 sq km after OALP-IX (won 9 blocks, >51,000 sq km, >47,000 offshore).
OALP-IX private/other winners: Vedanta (Cairn) 7 blocks; Reliance-BP 1 shallow-water block (BP's maiden India exploration licence).

OALP-X (launched Feb 2025): 25 blocks, ~192,000 sq km, 51% in previously restricted zones -- NOT yet awarded (bid deadline extended to 18 Feb 2026). OALP-XI: 21 blocks offered (2026) -- also a target/offer, not awarded.
caveats: Distinguish EXPLORATION acreage (blocks licensed to explore -- hundreds of thousands of sq km) from PRODUCING-FIELD SURFACE LAND (well pads/facilities -- a tiny fraction; offshore fields occupy no land at all).
Units: figures are in sq km. 1 sq km = 247.1 acres = 100 hectares.

The 1 million sq km 'under exploration by 2030' is a TARGET (Puri); the ~537,600 sq km (16% of 3.36M) is an end-2024 achieved/projected figure -- both differ from the ~1 million sq km of No-Go area that was administratively FREED (opened) for exploration.
ONGC's 162,701 sq km is as on 31-Mar-2023 and does NOT yet include OALP-IX (Apr-2025) additions; a post-IX consolidated ONGC total was not found.
No published single onshore/offshore split exists for cumulative OALP I-IX or for ONGC's total acreage; only the OALP-IX round is broken out by onland/shallow/deep.
Producing-field 'area' figures (Ankleshwar ~20 sq km, Mumbai High 65x23 km) are geological field extents, NOT the surface land footprint occupied by facilities -- do not treat them as land-use area.
OALP-X and OALP-XI blocks/areas are OFFERS, not awards -- exclude from 'awarded' totals.
unavailable: ONGC total exploration acreage broken into onshore vs offshore sq km (only aggregate 162,701 sq km found).
ONGC's consolidated total acreage AFTER OALP-IX (post Apr-2025).
Onshore/offshore split of cumulative OALP I-IX (378,651 sq km).
Per-company sq km area for ONGC and Vedanta OALP-IX wins (only block counts published; OIL's ~51,000 sq km and ONGC-OIL 10,964 sq km JV are the exceptions).

Actual SURFACE LAND (hectares) occupied by producing oilfields (well pads, GGS, terminals) for ONGC fields in Assam / Gujarat (Ankleshwar, Cambay) / KG basin -- not disclosed in accessible sources; only geological field extents available.
NELP legacy total awarded area as a single verified figure (NELP ran 9 rounds, 254 blocks; a clean total-area sq km figure was not retrieved in this session).
Mining-lease (PML) vs exploration-lease (PEL) current split for ONGC post-2020.

scale_framing.vs_industrial_land: OALP I-IX has licensed ~378,651 sq km (~93.6 MILLION acres) for exploration -- ~370x the ~253,700 acres of developed-park industrial land in the entire central IILB. BUT this is LICENCE AREA to explore, not siteable surface land: a block is a right to survey/drill within a polygon, and the actual land occupied by well pads/facilities is a tiny fraction; offshore blocks (90% of recent awards) occupy NO land at all. So exploration acreage is enormous as a category of government ALLOCATION but is NOT comparable to the industrial land bank as usable project land.
scale_framing.reuse_angle: The land-reuse opportunity is at the PRODUCING-FIELD SURFACE level, not the licence level: onshore fields that deplete (ONGC's mature Gujarat/Assam assets) leave behind already-cleared, already-connected surface land + well-pad infrastructure that can revert to other industrial use -- the subject of the user's separate oilfield-land-reuse legal analysis (to be cross-checked). Offshore depletion frees no land. Surface footprints are mostly undisclosed in accessible sources -- Ankleshwar field extent ~20 sq km, Mumbai High zero land -- so the reversion pool cannot be sized from public data yet.
